• Reranker adds $0.002/query
• Monitoring: ~$20/mo (Langfuse/LangSmith)
• Cold start: first query 2–5x slower
• Embedding model upgrade: must re-embed entire corpus

Interview tip: always mention hidden costs when discussing RAG architecture.

analogies.bridgeText: Connection pooling avoids the overhead of creating a new database connection per request by reusing existing connections. An embedding cache avoids the overhead of computing embeddings for repeated queries by reusing pre-computed vectors. Both sacrifice memory for speed by keeping expensive-to-create resources ready for immediate reuse.
analogies.breakPoint: Connection pools hold a fixed number of identical, interchangeable connections. Embedding caches hold query-specific vectors that are only useful for exact or near-exact query matches. A connection pool with 20 connections serves thousands of different queries; an embedding cache entry serves only the one query it was computed for (unless you implement semantic caching with similarity thresholds).

analogies.bridgeText: The N+1 problem fires a separate SQL query for each related record (1 query for users + N queries for each user's orders). Per-chunk embedding fires a separate API call for each document chunk. The fix is the same in both cases: batch. SQL uses JOINs or DataLoader; embedding APIs accept batches of up to 2048 texts per call, reducing API overhead from N calls to N/2048 calls.
analogies.breakPoint: The N+1 fix (JOIN/DataLoader) is purely a performance optimization — the results are identical. Batch embedding can differ from sequential embedding in some models due to padding and truncation behavior in batched tokenization. Always verify that batched embeddings match sequential ones for your model.

analogies.bridgeText: Tree-shaking removes unused code from your bundle — you only ship what is actually imported. Embedding cost reduction removes unchanged documents from re-indexing — you only re-embed documents that actually changed. Both audit what is needed and eliminate redundant work. Track document hashes to detect changes, just as bundlers track import graphs.
analogies.breakPoint: Tree-shaking is deterministic and automated by the bundler. Document change detection requires you to build and maintain a hash-tracking system (SHA-256 per document → compare on re-index). Also, tree-shaking runs at build time (once); document hash checking runs continuously in production.

analogies.bridgeText: HTML srcset serves different image resolutions to different devices — a phone gets 480px, a desktop gets 1920px. Same image, different quality-size tradeoffs. Matryoshka embeddings serve different dimension counts for different use cases — a mobile app gets 256 dims (fast, small), a production API gets 1536 dims (precise, larger). Same model, different quality-cost tradeoffs.
analogies.breakPoint: srcset requires storing multiple image files (each resolution is a separate file). Matryoshka requires storing only the full-dimension vector — you truncate at query time to the desired dimension. This makes Matryoshka more storage-efficient than the srcset analogy suggests.

analogies.bridgeText: Spot instances run non-urgent workloads at 60-90% discount by using spare capacity. Batch embedding APIs (OpenAI Batch API) process non-urgent embeddings at 50% discount by running them during off-peak hours. Both trade latency for cost — you submit the work and get results hours later at a fraction of the real-time price.
analogies.breakPoint: Spot instances can be interrupted mid-job (you need checkpointing). Batch embedding APIs guarantee completion — they just take longer (up to 24 hours). Spot instances have variable pricing; batch embedding has a fixed discount (typically 50%).

analogies.bridgeText: A CDN caches responses at edge locations — if someone nearby already requested the same URL, the cached response is served instantly. A semantic cache caches RAG responses — if someone already asked a semantically similar question, the cached answer is returned instantly. Both eliminate redundant computation for repeated or similar requests.
analogies.breakPoint: CDN caching uses exact URL matching — '/api/users/123' either hits the cache or misses. Semantic caching uses similarity thresholds — 'how do I reset my password' and 'password reset help' should hit the same cache entry. Setting the similarity threshold is tricky: too strict (0.98) = few cache hits; too loose (0.85) = stale/wrong cached answers for different-enough queries.

content.question: You implement a semantic cache for your RAG pipeline. Cache hit rate is 60%, which cuts costs dramatically. But users report that cached answers are sometimes stale — they reference outdated documentation. Your documents are re-indexed weekly. What is the issue?
options: The semantic similarity threshold is too low — it is matching dissimilar queries
The cache TTL must match the re-indexing interval — cached answers expire when documents change
Semantic caching is fundamentally incompatible with frequently updated documents
The embedding model needs to be retrained on the new documents
content.reveal: The cache TTL must match the re-indexing interval. If documents are re-indexed weekly but cached answers live for 30 days, users get answers grounded in month-old documents. The fix: set cache TTL equal to (or shorter than) the re-indexing interval. If you re-index weekly, set cache TTL to 7 days. Better yet: implement cache invalidation — when a document is re-indexed, invalidate all cache entries whose retrieved chunks came from that document. This requires storing the source chunk IDs alongside the cached answer. Semantic caching works fine with updating documents, but only if the cache lifecycle is tied to the content lifecycle.

content.question: Your company spends $500/month on embedding API calls to keep the RAG knowledge base up to date. Only 10% of documents change each week, but you re-embed the entire corpus every re-index. Your boss wants to cut embedding costs by 80%. What is the most effective approach?
options: Switch to a cheaper embedding model (text-embedding-3-small at $0.02/M tokens vs $0.13/M)
Track document content hashes — only re-embed documents whose SHA-256 hash changed since last index (skip the 90% that are unchanged)
Reduce embedding dimensions from 1536 to 512 using Matryoshka truncation
Move to a monthly re-indexing schedule instead of weekly
content.reveal: Track document hashes. If only 10% of documents change per week, you are wasting 90% of your embedding budget re-computing identical vectors. Implement: (1) compute SHA-256 hash of each document's content, (2) store hashes alongside embeddings, (3) on re-index, compare new hash to stored hash — only re-embed if different. This cuts costs by ~90% (from $500 to ~$50). Switching models cuts cost-per-token but still wastes money on unchanged documents. Reducing dimensions does not reduce API calls. Monthly re-indexing delays knowledge updates by 3 weeks. The hash-tracking approach is used by production systems at Notion, Stripe, and Intercom.

levels.title: Interview Gauntlet
levels.subtitle: 10 questions. Every senior AI engineer is asked these.
levels.hook: You've built the knowledge. Now prove it under pressure. These are the exact questions asked at FAANG, top-tier AI companies, and forward-thinking enterprises when hiring Senior AI Engineers. No partial credit. Clock is running.

questions.question: A colleague says 'our RAG faithfulness score is 0.72 — that's acceptable.' What's your response?
options: Agree — 0.72 is above the 0.70 industry minimum
Challenge — 0.72 means 28% of answers contain claims not in retrieved docs. Production minimum is 0.80.
Ask for context — depends on the use case and domain
Request a longer evaluation dataset before judging
questions.explanation: 0.72 faithfulness means 28% of answers contain fabricated claims unsupported by retrieved context. In any customer-facing or compliance-sensitive application, that is unacceptable. The production minimum is 0.80. 'Acceptable' is a red flag — this needs to be a deployment blocker.

questions.question: You're tasked with adding hybrid search to an existing pgvector-only RAG system. What's the minimum viable change?
options: Add a separate Elasticsearch instance for BM25
Enable pg_trgm extension + GIN index on content column, add BM25 CTE, merge with RRF in the query — no new infra
Replace pgvector with Qdrant which has built-in hybrid search
Use OpenAI's embedding model which includes BM25 by default
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL's full-text search (tsvector + GIN index) provides BM25-equivalent lexical search. Add a GIN index, write a hybrid query with CTEs for both retrieval paths, merge with RRF. Zero new infrastructure. The same Postgres instance handles both vector AND lexical search.

questions.question: Your RAG system context precision is 0.4. What does this mean and what should you fix?
options: 40% of your answers are correct — improve the LLM prompt
Only 40% of retrieved chunks are relevant — fix chunking quality or add metadata filtering
40% of queries are being answered — increase the number of documents
The embedding model is only 40% accurate — upgrade to a better model
questions.explanation: Context precision = fraction of retrieved chunks that are actually relevant to the query. 0.4 means 60% of what you're feeding to the LLM is noise — this explains hallucination and off-topic answers. Fix: improve chunking (smaller, more focused chunks), add metadata filtering to narrow retrieval, or switch to hybrid search for better initial recall.

questions.question: You need to add RAG to a system where data is too sensitive to send to any external API. Budget is $500/month.
options: This is impossible — RAG requires cloud API for embeddings
Self-host: Qwen3-Embedding on a $20/mo GPU + pgvector + self-hosted Ollama for generation. Total: ~$40/mo
Use Azure OpenAI — same models but data stays in your tenant
Use client-side encryption before sending to the API
questions.explanation: Fully self-hosted RAG is viable and cheap: Qwen3-Embedding on a single GPU (~$20/mo on Lambda Labs), pgvector (free PostgreSQL extension), Ollama for generation (self-hosted, Llama-3.1-8B quality is production-grade for most tasks). Total < $50/month. Azure OpenAI is a valid option too but costs 5-10x more.

questions.question: What is the correct distance metric for normalized text embeddings in pgvector?
options: <-> (L2/Euclidean distance)
<=> (cosine distance)
<#> (inner product)
All three give identical results for normalized vectors
questions.explanation: <=> is cosine distance. For normalized vectors, cosine and inner product are equivalent, but <=> makes the semantic clearer. <-> is L2/Euclidean — it measures geometric distance and gives different results than cosine for unnormalized vectors. Most embedding models output normalized vectors, so <=> is the correct default.

levels.key_insight: You've mastered Production RAG. The differentiators: you can diagnose retrieval vs generation failures, defend architectural tradeoffs with real numbers, and design cost-optimized pipelines. Chapter 2 unlocked: Local SLMs with Ollama — run AI at $0/month on your own hardware.

content.markdown:
You've built and optimized a production RAG pipeline from first principles. Here's what you now know at a senior engineer level:

| Concept | Interview-Ready Answer |
|---------|----------------------|
| **RAG vs Fine-tuning** | RAG for dynamic knowledge + citations. Fine-tuning for style + classification. |
| **Chunking** | RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter at 512 tokens, 50 overlap, always parse HTML/PDF first. |
| **Embeddings** | Self-host BGE-M3 at scale. text-embedding-3-small for startups. Matryoshka at 512 dims. |
| **Vector DB** | pgvector for <100M vectors if you run Postgres. Pinecone for serverless billion-scale. |
| **Hybrid Search** | Dense + BM25 + RRF. Pushes precision from 62% to 84%. Zero extra infra on Postgres. |
| **Re-ranking** | Conditional skip at confidence > 0.85. Saves 63% of cross-encoder calls. |
| **Evaluation** | RAGAS faithfulness >= 0.80 as hard gate. Cross-family judge. Run on every PR. |
| **Cost** | Semantic cache (#1 lever), model routing (#2), Matryoshka dims (#3). |

These 8 concepts cover 90% of Senior AI Engineer RAG interview questions.

content.title: The RAG Chapter Interview Signal
content.content:
**Level 1 signal:** Can distinguish RAG from fine-tuning. Knows RAG doesn't retrain the model.

**Level 2 signal:** Knows chunking matters more than embedding model. Mentions metadata.

**Level 3 signal:** Can calculate self-hosting break-even point. Mentions Matryoshka truncation.

**Level 4 signal:** Chose pgvector over Pinecone for their existing Postgres setup. Knows HNSW.

**Level 5 signal:** Knows cosine (<=>) vs L2 (<->). Can write the RRF SQL query from memory.

**Level 6 signal:** Invented conditional re-ranking in their system. Mentions ColBERT.

**Level 7 signal:** Blocks on faithfulness < 0.80. Uses cross-family judge. CI/CD on every PR.

**Level 8 signal:** Chose Standard RAG first and only added GraphRAG when theme queries failed.

Senior = can defend every architectural choice with production numbers and failure modes.
